perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information shall be held in stri ct confidence by Receiving Party and shall not be used by Receiving Party except for (i) the pur poses described in this Agreement, unless disclosure or further use is authorized or consented to in writing by Disclosing Party which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld or dela yed and/or (ii) the case where disclosure of the same is required under applicable law or by a gove rnmental order, rule or regulation or by the regulations of any relevant stock exchange or other governmental authority (provided that the Receiving party shall give written notice of such re quired disclosure to the other party prior to the disclosure).
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information shall be held with reasonable confidentiality by Receiving Party and should not be used by Receiving Party except for (i) the purposes described in this Agreement, unless disclosure or further use is authorized or consented to in writing by Disclosing Party which consent may be withheld or delayed at their discretion and/or (ii) the case where disclosure of the same is arguably required under applicable law or by a governmental order, rule or regulation or by the regulations of any relevant stock exchange or other governmental authority (provided that the Receiving party will attempt to give written notice of such required disclosure to the other party prior to the disclosure).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable confidentiality' weakens the obligation. Allowing the Disclosing Party to withhold consent 'at their discretion' introduces arbitrariness. Changing 'required' to 'arguably required' makes the disclosure requirement subjective. Replacing 'shall give written notice' with 'will attempt to give written notice' further diminishes the obligation. These changes introduce ambiguity and weaken the protections afforded to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the DUTSA, which protects trade secrets. By changing 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable confidentiality' and allowing arbitrary withholding of consent, the contract fails to provide adequate protection for trade secrets, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation under the DUTSA. The ambiguity introduced makes enforcing the confidentiality provisions harder, going against the intent of the DUTSA to safeguard trade secrets effectively.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of trade secrets, contradicting the intent of trade secret law. The original contract required 'strict confidence,' while the revised version mandates only 'reasonable confidentiality,' thereby diminishing the legal safeguard. The DUTSA protects trade secrets, defining 'misappropriation' as the 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent' (Snippet 1). By weakening confidentiality obligations and granting discretionary consent, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation and renders it more difficult to enforce confidentiality obligations, which is the core of trade secret protection. The change from 'required' to 'arguably required' further weakens obligations related to lawful disclosure. Furthermore, 'shall give written notice' becomes 'will attempt to give written notice,' also eroding a critical obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon a request by the Disclosing Pa rty at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Inform ation of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Inform ation and any and all copies or extracts thereof or upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that all materials containing Proprietary Information (incl uding all copies thereof) have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will endeavor to return Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and documents containing such Proprietary Information and copies thereof, or upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will make an effort to certify in writing that materials containing Proprietary Information have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy Proprietary Information. Replacing 'Immediately upon a request' with 'Upon reasonable request' introduces subjectivity and a delay. Changing 'will turn over' to 'will endeavor to return' makes the return of information less certain. Changing 'shall certify' to 'will make an effort to certify' weakens the certification requirement, which implies a less strict obligation. The use of 'endeavor' and 'make an effort' makes the obligation uncertain, and compliance can be argued.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2004 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation. This modification undermines the ability to obtain injunctive relief as the returning or destroying confidential information is not guaranteed. This makes it harder to recover the proprietary information, thus weakening trade secret protection under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligations regarding the return or destruction of Proprietary Information, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The original text mandates specific actions ('will turn over', 'shall certify'), while the modified text introduces uncertainty with phrases like 'endeavor to return' and 'make an effort to certify.' The Law Explanation states that this undermines the ability to obtain injunctive relief for misappropriation under Section 2004 as the returning or destroying confidential information is not guaranteed. The modification introduces subjectivity, potentially making it difficult to enforce trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By weakening the obligation to return or destroy, the modification increases the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the intent of trade secret protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware, without regard to conflicts of law principles. Any dispute, controversy and/or difference which may arise between the Parties out of or in relation to or in connection with this Agreement, or the breach thereof, which cannot be settled by amicable mutual accord without undue delay, shall be filed in the courts of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware, potentially considering conflicts of law principles. Any dispute, controversy and/or difference which may arise between the Parties in connection with this Agreement, or an alleged breach thereof, which cannot be settled through what each party deems to be reasonable attempts at amicable mutual accord, may be subject to mediation before potentially being filed in courts, location unspecified.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces multiple ambiguities. Changing 'shall be governed' to 'may be governed' makes the governing law uncertain. Adding 'potentially considering conflicts of law principles' adds further uncertainty. Changing 'breach thereof' to 'alleged breach thereof' introduces a layer of skepticism. Replacing 'amicable mutual accord without undue delay' with 'what each party deems to be reasonable attempts at amicable mutual accord' introduces subjectivity. Removing the mandatory filing in Delaware courts and replacing it with 'may be subject to mediation before potentially being filed in courts, location unspecified' eliminates a clear forum selection clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding Contract Certainty and Forum Selection
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications undermine the certainty and enforceability of the contract, particularly regarding dispute resolution, which Delaware law favors. By introducing ambiguity regarding the governing law and forum, the modified text contradicts Delaware's preference for clear and enforceable forum selection clauses and contractual provisions. Section 2708 aims to ensure business agreements in Delaware are predictable and reliable, but the changes work against this principle.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that directly contradict the original contract's clarity and enforceability, particularly regarding dispute resolution. The modification makes the governing law uncertain by changing 'shall be governed' to 'may be governed'. The original text mandated filing disputes in Delaware courts, but the changed text replaces it with 'may be subject to mediation before potentially being filed in courts, location unspecified,' eliminating a clear forum selection clause. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily addresses criminal procedure, the Law Explanation suggests that Delaware law favors clear contractual provisions, and the changes undermine this principle by creating ambiguities about governing law and dispute resolution processes.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that this Agreement is severable and that in the event any provision of this Agreement is held to be ille gal, invalid or enforceab le, the legality, validity and enforceability of the remaining provisi ons will not be affected or impaired.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties believe that this Agreement is potentially severable, and in the hypothetical event that a provision of this Agreement is somehow deemed illegal, possibly invalid, or arguably unenforceable, the effect on the legality, validity, and enforceability of the remaining provisions is unclear.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the severability clause by introducing uncertainty. Replacing 'agree that this Agreement is severable' with 'believe that this Agreement is potentially severable' makes the severability less certain. Using 'hypothetical event,' 'somehow deemed,' 'possibly invalid,' and 'arguably unenforceable' adds layers of doubt. Stating that 'the effect on the legality, validity, and enforceability of the remaining provisions is unclear' further undermines the clause's intent, which introduce ambiguities and increase the likelihood of disputes regarding the contract's enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles of Severability in Delaware
perturbation.law_citation: Enforceability under Delaware Contract Law (Common Law Principles)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law doesn't have a specific statute dictating contract severability, the common law principles heavily favor enforcing the intent of the parties to the greatest extent possible. By weakening the severability clause, the modifications cast doubt on the parties' intent, potentially jeopardizing the entire contract if one provision is challenged. This goes against the general principle of upholding contracts whenever possible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity into the severability clause, undermining its intended effect. The original text states the parties 'agree that this Agreement is severable,' providing a clear statement of intent. The changed text weakens this by saying the parties 'believe that this Agreement is potentially severable,' which introduces uncertainty. The addition of terms like 'hypothetical event,' 'somehow deemed,' 'possibly invalid,' and 'arguably unenforceable' further increases doubt, making the enforceability of the severability clause questionable. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, 'A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law.' By casting doubt on the severability of the agreement, the changes introduce ambiguities and increase the likelihood of disputes regarding the contract's enforceability, potentially jeopardizing the entire contract if one provision is challenged. The change creates a legal contradiction because the ambiguous language directly undermines the enforceability of a standard legal clause.
